mapQuestions.question: What sea surrounds Greece?
mapQuestions.answer: The Mediterranean, Aegean, and Ionian seas all touch Greece. 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Greece?
mapQuestions.answer: Athens — one of the oldest cities in the world, lived in continuously for 7,000 years.

mapQuestions.question: Roughly how many Greek islands are there?
mapQuestions.answer: About 6,000 islands and islets, though only around 230 are inhabited.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Greece?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Olympus (2,917 m) — the ancient Greeks believed their gods lived on top.

foods.name: Gyros
foods.description: Meat roasted on a vertical spit, sliced into pita with tomato, onion, and tzatziki. Greek fast food at its best.

foods.name: Feta
foods.description: Crumbly salty white cheese, made from sheep's milk. Goes on every salad in the country.

foods.name: Greek salad (Horiatiki)
foods.description: Tomato, cucumber, onion, olives, big chunk of feta on top, drizzled with olive oil and oregano. No lettuce — that's the foreigner version.

foods.name: Olives & olive oil
foods.description: Greece has 130 million olive trees — that's 13 trees per person. Greek olive oil is some of the best in the world.

foods.name: Loukoumades
foods.description: Little doughnut balls, fried golden, then drizzled with honey, cinnamon, and nuts. Often eaten warm.

foods.name: Greek coffee (frappé)
foods.description: Iced instant coffee shaken into a foam. Invented by accident at the 1957 Thessaloniki trade fair when a Nestlé rep couldn't find hot water.

scavengerItems.label: A column from a ruined temple
An olive tree older than your grandparents
A pebbly beach with crystal-clear water
Octopus hanging up to dry outside a taverna
A small fishing boat painted bright colours
A house with bright blue shutters
Strings of dried tomatoes or peppers hanging from a wall
Old men playing backgammon outside a café

geoMatches.description: The capital. The Acropolis (rocky hill in the centre) holds the Parthenon, built 2,500 years ago.
A volcanic island shaped like a half-circle, with white houses and blue domes on cliffs above the sea.
The biggest Greek island, with mountains, beaches, ancient palaces, and its own cuisine.
The mythical home of the gods. Still snow-capped most of the year and climbable in two days.
Massive stone pillars with monasteries built on top — monks used to be hauled up in nets.

senses.smell: Wild oregano, olive oil, lemon, sea salt, charcoal grills, melon stalls…
senses.hear: Cicadas in the heat, church bells echoing across hills, "Yamas!" toasts, donkeys braying…
senses.taste: Salty feta, sweet honey, sharp lemon, juicy ripe tomatoes, briny olives…
senses.feel: Warm flat stones on bare feet, cold sea on hot skin, dry hilltop wind, soft sand…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is the Parthenon famous?
stories.body: The Parthenon was built 2,500 years ago on the Acropolis — a giant rocky hill in the middle of Athens. It was a temple to Athena, the goddess of wisdom and the city's protector. It was the most ambitious building of its time: 46 columns, perfectly engineered using maths the Greeks invented (golden ratio, optical illusions to look straight). The ancient Greeks invented democracy, theatre, philosophy, the Olympics, and so much more right under its shadow. Even broken, the Parthenon is the most copied building in history — banks, museums, parliaments around the world look like it.

stories.question: Why were the original Olympic Games such a big deal?
stories.body: The Olympics started in Olympia in 776 BC — almost 3,000 years ago. They were held every four years for over 1,000 years. Athletes competed nude (the word "gymnastics" comes from the Greek word for "naked"). Wars between city-states would actually stop during the Games — a sacred truce called "Ekecheiria" let athletes and spectators travel safely. Winners got a wreath of olive leaves and lifetime fame. The modern Olympics began in 1896, again in Greece, restarting a tradition more than 2,500 years old.
stories.thinkingQuestion: In ancient times wars stopped for the Olympics. Should modern wars stop for big events too?

stories.question: Why are so many Greek houses painted white with blue?
stories.body: Greek islands look the way they do for two reasons. White paint reflects the hot summer sun and keeps houses cool — important when temperatures hit 40°C and there's no air conditioning. The blue trim has older roots: Greeks have been using a blue paint mixed from limestone for centuries because it lasts in the salty sea air. After a cholera outbreak in 1938 the government required every house on the islands to be whitewashed (white paint with lime kills bacteria). The blue-white look stuck, and now it's the most photographed style of architecture in the world.

stories.question: Who built the Palace of Knossos?
stories.body: About 4,000 years ago a civilisation called the Minoans lived on Crete. They were Europe's first advanced society — with flushing toilets, multi-storey palaces, beautiful painted frescoes, and writing. Their main palace at Knossos was a maze of 1,300 rooms, which is probably where the Greek myth of the Minotaur (half-man, half-bull) and his labyrinth came from. The Minoans collapsed mysteriously around 1450 BC — possibly because of a huge volcanic eruption on Santorini, which would have caused tsunamis and ash that killed crops.

stories.question: Why does Greek have its own alphabet — and where did our alphabet come from?
stories.body: The Greek alphabet was invented around 800 BC and is the ancestor of our Latin alphabet, the Cyrillic alphabet (Russian), and many others. The word "alphabet" itself comes from the first two Greek letters — alpha (α) and beta (β). When the Romans adopted the Greek alphabet they changed some letters to fit Latin, and that's what we use today. So every letter you read is a great-great-great-grandchild of Greek symbols invented almost 3,000 years ago.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Look at any word — it was written using symbols invented 3,000 years ago. What other everyday things might be older than you think?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Greece so far?
Greek islands or mainland — which would you choose for a longer stay?
Greeks eat dinner at 10pm. Could you get used to that?
If you had to live on one Greek island forever, which would it be?
What's one Greek habit you'd like to bring home with you?
Most ideas in Western science, art, and politics came from ancient Greece. What's one Greek idea you actually use every day?

wordSearch.hint: Greek foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Greek street cat
animals.description: On every island, every taverna terrace, every harbour wall. Locals feed them.

animals.name: Mountain goat
animals.description: Free-roaming on the islands and mainland hills — sometimes on rooftops.

animals.name: Donkey
animals.description: Still used on Santorini and Hydra to carry goods up steep staircases. Animal welfare worth checking before riding.

animals.name: Octopus
animals.description: Hanging up to dry outside seaside tavernas — also alive in rock pools if you snorkel.

animals.name: Pelican (Petros family)
animals.description: Wild pelicans famous in Mykonos town — adopted by locals since the 1950s.

animals.name: Loggerhead turtle
animals.description: Nesting on Zakynthos beaches between June and October. Strict protections in place.

animals.name: Mediterranean monk seal
animals.description: One of the world's rarest seals — about 700 left, mostly in the Greek Aegean.
